title: Getting a soil test for your pasture
subtitle: How to sample, where to send it, and what the results mean.
excerpt: A soil test for pasture measures pH, phosphorus, potassium, and magnesium, the four factors that most commonly limit grass productivity on smallholdings. The result tells you whether to lime, what fertiliser to apply, and whether the pasture is capable of supporting the stocking rate you intend.
sourceNotes: AHDB Nutrient Management Guide (RB209), ahdb.org.uk. Rothamsted Research soil health guidance. NVZ (Nitrate Vulnerable Zone) management rules, gov.uk.

glossaryTerms.definition: A measure of soil acidity or alkalinity on a scale of 1 to 14. A pH of 6.0 to 6.5 is ideal for productive pasture in the UK. Below 5.5, grass struggles and weeds take over.
A system for reporting soil nutrient levels from 0 (very deficient) to 4+ (very high). Index 2 is the target for productive pasture.

content.text: Pasture that is not producing as much grass as expected is often limited by

or one of four key nutrients rather than by the inherent quality of the land. A soil test costs roughly the same as a bag of lime and tells you whether lime is what you actually need before you buy it.

Sample in spring or autumn when the soil is not frozen and moisture is at a normal seasonal level. Avoid sampling immediately after applying fertiliser or lime, wait at least three months after any soil amendments before testing again.

A single sample represents a single field or management unit. Do not mix samples from different fields or from clearly different areas of the same field (a wet corner and a dry hilltop should be sampled separately).
Walk a W-pattern across the field and take a core sample at twenty to twenty-five points along the route.
Use a soil auger or spade to take each sample to 75 mm depth (the root zone for grass).
Combine all the cores in a clean bucket and mix thoroughly. Take approximately 500 g of the mixed sample for submission.
Place the sample in a clean plastic bag labelled with field name, date, and the test you require (pH, P, K, Mg, the standard pasture panel).
Testing labs include NRM Laboratories, ADAS, and Yara (UK). Merchants and agronomists often offer this service too. A standard panel is cheap; a full written report costs more but includes advice.
Results are shown on a standard

. For productive pasture, aim for pH 6.0 to 6.5, phosphorus index 2, potassium index 2, and magnesium index 2. A low pH is fixed with ground limestone. Nutrient shortfalls are corrected with specific fertiliser or slurry.

Most unimproved UK permanent pasture has a phosphorus surplus from decades of fertiliser application and manure spreading. Before buying phosphate fertiliser, the test often shows the real problem is pH, lime costs less and is usually the first fix.
